plainEnglish: Reporting after a campaign ends that reconciles what was delivered against what was bought.
whyItMatters: Where under-delivery, makegoods and audience shortfalls are settled; the close-out of the buy.

plainEnglish: An updated delivery projection based on actual performance to date.
whyItMatters: Lets you course-correct mid-flight - spot a shortfall early and add weight or shift budget.
practitionerNotes: Ad-ops trade term; no single canonical page.

plainEnglish: The period after launch/edit when the algorithm gathers data to stabilise delivery.
whyItMatters: Performance is unstable and often worse during it - don't judge or edit too soon. Meta needs ~50 optimisation events in 7 days to exit.

sources.label: Meta Business Help - About the learning phase

plainEnglish: When an ad set can't get enough optimisation events to ever exit the learning phase.
whyItMatters: It never stabilises, so performance stays volatile - usually fix by consolidating, widening, or choosing an earlier-funnel event.

sources.label: Meta Business Help - About the learning phase

plainEnglish: A change significant enough to send an ad set back into the learning phase.
whyItMatters: Edits to budget, audience, creative or optimisation can reset learning - batch changes to avoid resetting repeatedly.

sources.label: Meta Business Help - Significant edits & learning phase

plainEnglish: The specific action the algorithm is told to optimise toward (e.g. Purchase, Lead).
whyItMatters: Bidding chases this event - choose one with enough volume to learn, or you end up learning-limited.

plainEnglish: The primary goal a campaign is set up to optimise toward (awareness, traffic, conversions, etc.).
whyItMatters: Sets what the algorithm and the metrics are judged on - the objective should mirror the brief.

sources.label: Meta Business Help - About performance goals

plainEnglish: Making changes/decisions at the campaign level (budget, objective, structure).
whyItMatters: Broad levers that affect everything beneath - usually where budget allocation and strategy live.

plainEnglish: Tuning at the individual keyword level (bids, match types, negatives, pausing).
whyItMatters: Granular control to cut waste and back winners - the day-to-day of search management.

sources.label: Google Ads Help - Search terms report

plainEnglish: Optimising based on the actual search terms that triggered ads.
whyItMatters: The search-terms report reveals reality vs intent - the source of new keywords and negatives.

sources.label: Google Ads Help - Search terms report

plainEnglish: Finding new keywords to add by reviewing the search-terms report.
whyItMatters: Turns real queries into new, relevant keywords - a steady source of incremental, qualified traffic.

sources.label: Google Ads Help - Search terms report

plainEnglish: When multiple keywords in an account can trigger on the same query.
whyItMatters: Causes internal competition and muddled data - manage with negatives and clear structure.

sources.label: Google Ads Help - Negative keyword ideas

plainEnglish: Keywords (or campaigns) competing against each other for the same query.
whyItMatters: You bid against yourself, inflating cost and confusing optimisation - fix with negatives and tighter structure.
practitionerNotes: PPC concept; no Google Ads Help page uses this exact label.

plainEnglish: The underlying goal behind a search query (to learn, to find a site, to compare, to buy).
whyItMatters: Matching ad and landing page to intent is what converts; bidding on the wrong-intent terms wastes spend.
practitionerNotes: SEO/search concept; no Google canonical page - explainer-sourced.

plainEnglish: A search aimed at learning or researching ('how to', 'what is').
whyItMatters: Upper-funnel - great for content and awareness, weak for direct-response bidding.

plainEnglish: A search aimed at reaching a specific brand or site.
whyItMatters: Often your own brand terms - high conversion, but check you're not paying for clicks you'd win organically.

plainEnglish: A search showing buying intent or research toward a purchase ('best', 'price', 'buy').
whyItMatters: Lower-funnel and high-value - the terms most worth bidding aggressively on.

plainEnglish: A Quality Score component: Google's prediction of how likely your ad is to be clicked.
whyItMatters: Relevance and compelling copy lift it; a low rating flags ads that aren't resonating.

sources.label: Google Ads Help - About Quality Score

plainEnglish: A Quality Score component: how closely your ad matches the intent of the keyword.
whyItMatters: Tightly themed ad groups and matching copy improve it - and lower your CPCs.

sources.label: Google Ads Help - About Quality Score

plainEnglish: A Quality Score component: how useful and relevant your landing page is to the visitor.
whyItMatters: Speed, relevance and usability lift it; a poor page drags down Quality Score (and conversions).

sources.label: Google Ads Help - About Quality Score

plainEnglish: The point where each extra dollar of spend produces less incremental result.
whyItMatters: The saturation curve - knowing where a channel saturates is how you decide when to stop adding and diversify.

plainEnglish: How much search volume/results change when you change spend.
whyItMatters: Tells you whether more budget would buy more results, or just bid up the same clicks (low elasticity = saturated).
practitionerNotes: Marketing-economics concept; no single canonical page.

plainEnglish: The client-level entity in a DSP/ad platform that holds campaigns and billing.
whyItMatters: The top of the hierarchy - where access, billing and brand-level settings live.

plainEnglish: A buyer's account/identity within a DSP used to transact.
whyItMatters: Deals and inventory access are often tied to a specific seat ID - it identifies who's buying.

plainEnglish: The defined group of people a campaign is trying to reach.
whyItMatters: Everything starts here - the audience definition drives channel choice, targeting, creative and how success is measured.
practitionerNotes: Generic planning term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: A demographic group definition (e.g. People 25-54, Grocery Buyers).
whyItMatters: The trading shorthand for the target - TV and reach are bought and reported against a defined demo.

plainEnglish: The main audience a campaign is bought and optimised against.
whyItMatters: The number everyone is held to - the demo the plan's reach/frequency goals are set on.

plainEnglish: An additional audience tracked for reporting but not the main buying target.
whyItMatters: Shows spill/secondary value beyond the primary demo without changing the buy.

plainEnglish: A fictional, detailed profile representing a target customer, used to guide planning and creative.
whyItMatters: Makes an abstract audience feel real so messaging and channel choices stay human-centred.
practitionerNotes: Planning device; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: A defined subset of users grouped by a shared attribute or behaviour.
whyItMatters: The unit of targeting - good segmentation lets you tailor message and bid to the people most likely to respond.

plainEnglish: An audience built from your own first-party data (customer list, site/app visitors, engagers).
whyItMatters: Your most valuable, highest-converting audiences - the base for retargeting and lookalike seeds.

plainEnglish: A modelled audience of people similar to a source (seed) audience you provide.
whyItMatters: Scales prospecting by finding new people who resemble your best customers - quality depends on the seed.

plainEnglish: A modelled audience resembling a seed list of your existing customers/engagers.
whyItMatters: The standard way to find new, similar prospects at scale; a smaller, higher-quality seed usually beats a huge one.

plainEnglish: The source list of people a lookalike/modelled audience is built from.
whyItMatters: Quality in, quality out - a clean, high-value seed (e.g. best customers) makes a better lookalike than a broad one.
practitionerNotes: Defined within Meta's lookalike documentation.

plainEnglish: Meta's AI-driven audience that finds and expands the people most likely to convert (formerly automatic/expanded targeting).
whyItMatters: Hands audience-finding to Meta's automation - often outperforms manual targeting, with less control.

plainEnglish: Letting the algorithm reach people beyond your defined audience when it predicts they'll perform.
whyItMatters: Adds incremental reach/efficiency - but loosens control, so watch where delivery actually goes.

plainEnglish: Showing ads to people who previously engaged with your brand (visited the site, used the app, engaged a post).
whyItMatters: High-converting and cheap - but it harvests existing intent, so don't let it take all the credit (last-click trap).

sources.label: Google Ads Help - Your data / remarketing

plainEnglish: Google's term for retargeting - re-engaging past visitors with ads.
whyItMatters: Same concept as retargeting; Google now frames it under 'your data' segments. Efficient, but it captures demand rather than creating it.
practitionerNotes: Google has shifted from 'remarketing' to 'your data' terminology.

sources.label: Google Ads Help - Your data / remarketing

plainEnglish: Targeting new audiences who haven't engaged with your brand yet.
whyItMatters: Where growth comes from - harder and pricier than retargeting, and the truer test of a campaign's ability to create demand.
practitionerNotes: Generic term; Google 'audience segments' is the nearest primary.

plainEnglish: Audiences/users you explicitly tell a campaign not to target.
whyItMatters: Stops wasted spend (e.g. excluding existing customers from acquisition). Note: Meta is deprecating detailed-targeting exclusions.
practitionerNotes: Meta has been winding back detailed-targeting exclusions.

sources.label: Meta Business Help - Customer list audiences

plainEnglish: Using a list to exclude specific people (e.g. current customers, recent buyers) from seeing ads.
whyItMatters: Protects budget and experience - don't pay to acquire people you already have, or annoy recent purchasers.

sources.label: Meta Business Help - Customer list audiences

plainEnglish: Targeting by attributes like age, gender, location, income or parental status.
whyItMatters: Foundational but blunt - broad demos waste reach on the wrong people; combine with intent/interest for precision.

plainEnglish: Targeting people based on declared or inferred interests (e.g. fitness, travel).
whyItMatters: Reaches relevant mindsets at scale on social; broad-but-relevant, so pair with strong creative and measurement.

plainEnglish: Targeting based on past behaviours (purchases, device use, activities).
whyItMatters: Behaviour often predicts intent better than demographics - but signal loss is shrinking what's available.

plainEnglish: Targeting (or excluding) specific geographic locations.
whyItMatters: Concentrates spend where you operate/convert; also the basis of geo-lift incrementality tests.

plainEnglish: Targeting specific times of day or days of week (ad scheduling).
whyItMatters: Focus budget when your audience converts; quieten it when it doesn't.

plainEnglish: Targeting based on the content of the page/environment rather than the individual user.
whyItMatters: Privacy-resilient (no personal data) and back in favour as cookies fade; aligns ads with relevant content.

plainEnglish: Contextual targeting that matches ads to pages containing chosen keywords.
whyItMatters: A simple, privacy-safe way to put ads next to relevant content - precision depends on the keyword list.

plainEnglish: Contextual targeting by content category (using a standard content taxonomy).
whyItMatters: Targets whole topic areas brand-safely using shared category standards (e.g. IAB content taxonomy).

plainEnglish: Contextual targeting that reads the meaning of content, not just keywords.
whyItMatters: Smarter than keyword matching - understands nuance and avoids false matches (e.g. 'crash' in finance vs aviation).

plainEnglish: Matching the many identifiers a person uses (devices, emails, cookies) back to one individual.
whyItMatters: The backbone of cross-device targeting, frequency capping and measurement - and harder as signal loss bites.

plainEnglish: A known, logged-in identifier (e.g. a hashed email) that matches a person with certainty.
whyItMatters: The most accurate way to recognise someone across devices - the durable basis of identity as cookies fade.

plainEnglish: A modelled, statistical guess that links devices/signals to the same person without a logged-in match.
whyItMatters: Scales where deterministic data is missing, but is less accurate - good for reach, weaker for precise measurement.

plainEnglish: A map linking the various devices and identifiers that belong to the same person/household.
whyItMatters: What makes cross-device reach, frequency and measurement possible.

plainEnglish: Matching and sharing identifiers between platforms so they can recognise the same user.
whyItMatters: The plumbing that lets DSPs/SSPs/data partners agree on who's who - degraded by third-party cookie loss.

plainEnglish: Recognising and reaching the same person across their phone, laptop, TV and tablet.
whyItMatters: People switch screens constantly - cross-device is what keeps reach, frequency capping and attribution coherent.

plainEnglish: The media cost before agency/tech fees are netted out (the rate-card or booked cost).
whyItMatters: The top-line spend figure; what's 'working media' depends on what fees sit between gross and net.
practitionerNotes: Agency commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: The media cost after tech/platform fees are removed - closer to what reaches the publisher.
whyItMatters: The gap between gross and net is where ad-tech fees live; a key transparency number for clients.
practitionerNotes: Agency commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: Media cost including fees - the all-in figure billed before any rebates/discounts.
whyItMatters: What a client sees billed; understanding what's inside it (fees, tech) is the basis of fee transparency.
practitionerNotes: Agency commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: Media cost before the agency's fees are added.
whyItMatters: The base the agency fee/commission is calculated on; clarity on net vs gross avoids billing disputes.
practitionerNotes: Agency commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: The blended, realised rate actually paid after fees, discounts and added value.
whyItMatters: The true cost of the media - more honest than headline rates, which ignore rebates and value-adds.
practitionerNotes: Agency commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: A fixed or time-based charge for the agency's services (as opposed to a % commission).
whyItMatters: The transparent, decoupled-from-spend remuneration model clients increasingly prefer over commission.
practitionerNotes: Agency commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: The agency's service charge - a fixed or time-based fee for planning, buying and managing media.
whyItMatters: How the agency is paid; fee-based (vs commission) decouples remuneration from spend, reducing conflicts of interest.
practitionerNotes: Agency commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: An agency's earnings calculated as a percentage of media spend.
whyItMatters: The traditional model - simple, but ties agency income to spend, which can misalign incentives. Often replaced by fees.
practitionerNotes: Agency commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: An ongoing, regular (usually monthly) fee for continuous agency services.
whyItMatters: Predictable income/cost for both sides; suits always-on relationships rather than one-off projects.
practitionerNotes: Agency commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: A fixed fee for a defined piece of work/scope.
whyItMatters: Clear cost for a clear deliverable; good for one-offs but needs a tight scope to avoid creep.
practitionerNotes: Agency commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: The agency's revenue left after its costs - what it actually keeps.
whyItMatters: The health of the commercial relationship; too thin and the agency under-resources, too fat and it's poor value.
practitionerNotes: Agency commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: An amount added to a cost price to produce the sell price (the margin added on top of cost).
whyItMatters: How some media/services are priced; transparency on markup is central to client-agency trust.
practitionerNotes: Agency commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: Revenue minus the direct cost of delivering it, before overheads.
whyItMatters: The first read of profitability on a piece of business; falling gross margin is an early warning sign.

plainEnglish: Profit left after all costs (direct and overhead) are taken out.
whyItMatters: The bottom-line profitability of the business/account - what's actually retained.

plainEnglish: Profit as a percentage of revenue.
whyItMatters: The headline measure of how profitable an account or the business is.

plainEnglish: The percentage of spend retained by an intermediary (platform, exchange, reseller) as it passes through.
whyItMatters: Each take rate in the chain reduces working media - the heart of supply-path transparency.

plainEnglish: The cumulative layer of technology and platform fees taken across the programmatic supply chain.
whyItMatters: The 'ad tech tax' - the gap between budget and working media; minimising it (via SPO) is a core efficiency lever.

plainEnglish: A fee charged by a DSP/platform for using its technology to buy media.
whyItMatters: Part of the fee stack between gross and net; transparency on tech fees is a client expectation.

plainEnglish: A charge for using audience/third-party data in targeting.
whyItMatters: Another layer in the fee stack; data costs can quietly erode working media if not tracked.

plainEnglish: A billing document issued by a supplier requesting payment for goods/services.
whyItMatters: The instrument that moves money; in Australia a valid tax invoice is required to claim GST credits.

plainEnglish: Recognising a cost (or revenue) when it's incurred/earned, even if not yet invoiced or paid.
whyItMatters: Keeps the books matched to the period the activity happened - essential for media that delivers before it's billed.

plainEnglish: Media that has been delivered but not yet invoiced.
whyItMatters: Recognised as a cost/liability in the period it ran, so the accounts reflect reality before the invoice arrives.

plainEnglish: A correction to a previous accrual estimate once actuals are known.
whyItMatters: Trues-up the books when the real invoice differs from what was estimated - a routine month-end reconciliation step.
practitionerNotes: Finance ops term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: The accounting rules for when revenue can be booked.
whyItMatters: Governs when an agency can recognise income (e.g. as media delivers vs when invoiced); AASB 15/IFRS 15 set the standard.

sources.label: AASB 15 - Revenue from contracts with customers

plainEnglish: Matching invoices against what was booked and what actually delivered.
whyItMatters: Catches over/under-delivery, billing errors and discrepancies - the financial close-out of a campaign.
practitionerNotes: Finance ops term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: The month in which spend is recognised and billed.
whyItMatters: Determines which period a cost lands in - important when a campaign straddles month-ends.
practitionerNotes: Finance ops term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: The planned breakdown of how a campaign's spend will be billed month by month.
whyItMatters: The basis of invoicing and cashflow forecasting; it's what finance reconciles delivery against.
practitionerNotes: Finance ops term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: Goods and Services Tax - Australia's 10% tax on most goods and services.
whyItMatters: Added to most media invoices; registered businesses charge it and claim credits, so quotes specify inc/ex GST.

plainEnglish: A price that already includes the 10% GST.
whyItMatters: Always confirm inc vs ex GST on quotes/budgets - a 10% misread blows a budget. Divide by 11 to find the GST portion.

plainEnglish: A price that excludes GST (GST is added on top).
whyItMatters: Media is often quoted ex GST; add 10% for the billed amount. Mixing inc/ex GST is a common budgeting error.

plainEnglish: Australian Business Number - a unique identifier for a business, used on invoices and contracts.
whyItMatters: Required to invoice and deal with other businesses; without a supplier's ABN, you may have to withhold tax.

plainEnglish: Insertion Order - the document that books media, setting out what runs, when, where and at what price.
whyItMatters: The contract for a media buy; the IAB/4As standard terms define the rights and obligations on both sides.

plainEnglish: Purchase Order - a document a client issues to authorise and reference spend.
whyItMatters: Many clients won't pay an invoice without a matching PO number; getting it up front avoids billing delays.
practitionerNotes: Commercial process term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: Media Booking Authority - an internal approval that authorises media spend before it's booked.
whyItMatters: The control that ties a booking to client approval and budget; the spine of agency spend governance.
practitionerNotes: Australian agency term; defined in-house (no public page).

plainEnglish: Statement of Work - the document defining the scope, deliverables and timing of an engagement.
whyItMatters: What you're contracted to deliver; a tight SOW prevents scope creep and disputes.
practitionerNotes: Commercial contract term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: Master Services Agreement - the overarching contract governing the client-agency relationship.
whyItMatters: Sets the standing terms (liability, IP, payment) so each SOW doesn't re-negotiate them.
practitionerNotes: Commercial contract term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: Service Level Agreement - agreed standards (e.g. response/turnaround times) the agency commits to.
whyItMatters: Makes 'good service' measurable and accountable; often tied to remuneration or review.
practitionerNotes: Commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: Non-Disclosure Agreement - a contract to keep shared information confidential.
whyItMatters: Standard before sharing sensitive briefs/data, especially during pitches.

plainEnglish: Terms and Conditions - the standard terms that apply to a service or media buy.
whyItMatters: Define rights, liabilities and remedies (e.g. makegoods, cancellation); the IAB/4As terms are the media standard.
practitionerNotes: Commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: Agency of Record - the agency formally appointed to handle a client's media/advertising.
whyItMatters: The lead, accountable agency relationship; the prize of a pitch and the basis of the MSA.
practitionerNotes: Commercial term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: The competitive process by which a client selects an agency.
whyItMatters: How accounts are won and lost; run well (per 4As/ANA principles) it's fair and efficient, run badly it's costly for all.

plainEnglish: Request for Proposal - a client's formal invitation for agencies to propose an approach and price.
whyItMatters: The brief that kicks off a pitch; the quality of the RFP shapes the quality of responses.

plainEnglish: Request for Quote - a request focused on pricing for a defined scope.
whyItMatters: Narrower than an RFP - used when the work is clear and the question is mainly cost.
practitionerNotes: Commercial process term; defined in-house.

plainEnglish: Advertising sold by retailers across their own sites, apps and audiences using their first-party shopper data.
whyItMatters: The fastest-growing channel - close to purchase, rich first-party data, closed-loop sales measurement, now extending to CTV/in-store.

plainEnglish: Amazon's advertising platform - Sponsored Products/Brands/Display plus its DSP, powered by shopper data.
whyItMatters: The largest retail-media network - captures intent at the point of purchase with closed-loop sales data.
